attributes.stem:
A 16-year-old girl presents with a four-week history of an itchy red patch on her arm. She appears embarrassed about the lesion and states that another student in the boarding house at school has a similar lesion on her back. She reports being well recently, with no significant medical history and no regular medications.

Examination reveals a singular round plaque with central hypopigmentation and a scaly, raised border (Image). There are no other areas of involvement, including the nails and scalp.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation is suggestive of tinea corporis, a superficial fungal infection of the skin likely caused by Trichophyton rubrum. It is commonly known as ringworm, as it presents with ring-shaped lesions that have central hypopigmentation and a raised scaly border. Risk factors include hyperhidrosis, diabetes, immunodeficiency, household crowding, occlusive clothing, and recreational activities involving close contact with others (as in this case). Tinea corporis should always be considered where there is a solitary patch or asymmetrical rash. Tinea corporis can be managed with general measures (clean, loose-fitting clothes, clean towels, avoid contact with others) and topical antifungal regimens, or oral antifungals if there is more extensive involvement.

Skin scraping would be the most appropriate initial investigation when suspecting tinea corporis. Skin scrapings with a potassium hydroxide (KOH) stain is a quick and inexpensive test that can be performed in the clinic to confirm the presence of a fungal infection (showing branched segmented hyphae). A fungal culture of skin scrapings is the best confirmatory test for tinea corporis. The culture result may take a few weeks, and then empirical treatment can be altered to match the results.

Skin swabs may be used as an alternative to skin scrapings for a suspected dermatophyte infection. If pustules are present, they may be de-roofed and swabbed for fungal microscopy and culture. However, skin swabs are not recommended as the first-line initial diagnostic test for fungal infections. A skin swab may also be used to swab a pustule where a secondary bacterial infection is suspected. This is more common in young children with atopic dermatitis and tinea corporis.

Skin biopsy with PAS staining (periodic acid-Schiff) may be beneficial to confirm a dermatophyte infection in specific circumstances; however, it is not the most appropriate initial diagnostic test. A skin biopsy would be a suitable first-line diagnostic tool for psoriasis or seborrhoeic dermatitis.

  • Seborrhoeic dermatitis usually presents with thin, pale or erythematous plaques that often have a yellow, greasy crust. It is more common on the scalp, face, and sometimes the upper trunk.

  • Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterised by clearly defined, scaly plaques that are typically salmon pink or silvery-white. They are usually located on the scalp, elbows, and knees, and there is often nail involvement.

HHV6 serology would be used to diagnose pityriasis rosea, a human-herpes virus 6 infection. It is a self-limiting rash that usually resolves in about six to ten weeks. It is usually preceded by flu-like symptoms (e.g., cough or sore throat). It is characterised by a large ‘herald patch’ and results in areas of hypopigmentation; within a few days to weeks, more scaly patches or plaques appear. It should be considered if fungal scraping returns negative.

Patch testing would be used to assess for allergic or contact dermatitis. Contact dermatitis is commonly caused by occupational exposures. Its presentation is highly variable but often includes redness, blisters, swelling, dryness or scaling, or changes to the pigmentation of the skin. Due to the lack of specific exposures identified in the patient’s history, this rash is unlikely to be caused by contact dermatitis.
learningPoints: A skin scraping with a potassium hydroxide stain is the most appropriate initial investigation when suspecting tinea corporis.
references: Kovitwanichkanont T, Chong A. Superficial Fungal Infections. Australian Journal of General Practice. Published October 2019, accessed May, 2023. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-05-19-4930

Goldstein A, Goldstein B. Dermatophyte (tinea) infections. UpToDate. Updated April, 2023. Accessed May, 2023. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/dermatophyte-tinea-infections?search=tinea%20corporis&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~77&usage_type=default&display_rank=1#H1219713189

Unwala R. Approach to the patient with annular skin lesions. UpToDate. Updated April, 2023. Accessed May, 2023. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/approach-to-the-patient-with-annular-skin-lesions?search=tinea%20corporis&source=search_result&selectedTitle=2~77&usage_type=default&display_rank=2
